reasoning_model_1: The model fails to generate the required non-linguistic vocalizations. Instead of producing the sound of laughter, it reads the text 'Hahaha' phonetically as 'Ha ha ha' (0:00-0:02). While the tone is somewhat bright, it lacks the physiological characteristics of laughter (breathiness, rhythmic bursts). Furthermore, the model hallucinates significant content not present in the prompt, adding the sentences 'I couldn't stop laughing. Seriously.' at the end (0:04-0:07). Due to the failure to produce the target feature and the presence of hallucinations, the performance is rated poorly.
reasoning_model_2: The model successfully generates high-quality non-linguistic vocalizations. From 0:00 to 0:04, it produces a very natural, breathless laugh that sounds genuinely human, effectively interpreting the 'Hahaha' cue as an instruction for sound generation rather than text to be read. However, the content accuracy is flawed; the model paraphrases the script significantly, changing 'that joke you told was so funny' to just 'That's so funny' (0:04-0:05), omitting the middle phrase. Despite the content error, it is the only model that achieves the core task of generating laughter.
model_comparison: Model 2 is the clear winner as it demonstrates the capability to generate the specific paralinguistic feature (Non-Linguistic Vocalizations/Laughter) requested by the user. Model 1 merely reads the onomatopoeia as text and hallucinates extra content, failing the core intent of the prompt. Although Model 2 has content accuracy issues (omission), its success in generating realistic laughter makes it superior in the context of Feature Control.
